annotator S.report_text:
POSHSPY's use of WMI to both store and persist the backdoor code makes it nearly invisible to anyone not familiar with the intricacies of WMI. Adversaries may abuse Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) to execute malicious commands and payloads.

Its use of a PowerShell payload means that only legitimate system processes are utilized and that the malicious code execution can only be identi#ed through enhanced logging or in memory. The use of PowerShell payloads allows only legitimate system processes to be used, making detection of malicious code more difficult.

WMI permanent event subscriptions can be used to trigger actions when speci#ed conditions are met. Creating a WMI event subscription to run the backdoor.

We have observed APT29 use WMI to persist a backdoor and also store the PowerShell backdoor code. Adversaries may attempt to make an executable or file difficult to discover or analyze by encrypting, encoding, or otherwise obfuscating its contents on the system or in transit.

APT29 wrote the encrypted and base64- encoded PowerShell backdoor code into that prope%y. Adversaries may abuse PowerShell commands and scripts for execution.

APT29 then created a WMI event subscription in order to execute the backdoor. Adversaries may establish persistence and elevate privileges by executing malicious content triggered by a Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) event subscription.

The subscription was con#gured to run a PowerShell command that read, decrypted, and executed the backdoor code directly from the new WMI prope%y. Adversaries may use Obfuscated Files or Information to hide artifacts of an intrusion from analysis. They may require separate mechanisms to decode or deobfuscate that information depending on how they intend to use it.

The subscription was con#gured to run a PowerShell command that read, decrypted, and executed the backdoor code directly from the new WMI prope%y. Adversaries may abuse PowerShell commands and scripts for execution.

This allowed them to install a persistent backdoor without leaving any a%ifacts on the system's hard drive, outside of the WMI repository. This "#leless" backdoor methodology made the identi#cation of the backdoor much more di$cult using standard host analysis techniques. Adversaries may attempt to hide artifacts associated with their behaviors to evade detection.

The WindowsParentalControlsMigration consumer was con#gured to silently execute a base64- encoded PowerShell command. Upon execution, this command extracted, decrypted, and executed the PowerShell backdoor payload stored in the HiveUploadTask text prope%y of the RacTask class. Adversaries may attempt to make an executable or file difficult to discover or analyze by encrypting, encoding, or otherwise obfuscating its contents on the system or in transit.

The WindowsParentalControlsMigration consumer was con#gured to silently execute a base64- encoded PowerShell command. Upon execution, this command extracted, decrypted, and executed the PowerShell backdoor payload stored in the HiveUploadTask text prope%y of the RacTask class. Adversaries may abuse PowerShell commands and scripts for execution.

The POSHSPY backdoor is designed to download and execute additional PowerShell code and Windows binaries. Adversaries may transfer tools or other files from an external system into a compromised environment.

Downloading and executing PowerShell code as an EncodedCommand Adversaries may attempt to make an executable or file difficult to discover or analyze by encrypting, encoding, or otherwise obfuscating its contents on the system or in transit.

Downloading and executing PowerShell code as an EncodedCommand Adversaries may abuse PowerShell commands and scripts for execution.

Writing executables to a randomly-selected directory under Program Files, and naming the EXE to match the chosen directory name, or, if that fails, writing the executable to a systemgenerated temporary #le name, using the EXE extension Adversaries may match or approximate the name or location of legitimate files or resources when naming/placing them.

Modifying the Standard Information timestamps (created, modi#ed, accessed) of every downloaded executable to match a randomly selected #le from the System32 directory that was created prior to 2013 Adversaries may modify file time attributes to hide new or changes to existing files. Timestomping is a technique that modifies the timestamps of a file (the modify, access, create, and change times), often to mimic files that are in the same folder..

Encrypting communications using AES and RSA public key cryptography to the use of asymmetric encryption algorithms, such as RSA, and symmetric encryption algorithms, such as AES, to protect communications, making it more difficult for defenders to monitor and intercept communications.

Deriving C2 URLs from a Domain Generation Algorithm (DGA) Adversaries may make use of Domain Generation Algorithms (DGAs) to dynamically identify a destination domain for command and control traffic rather than relying on a list of static IP addresses or domains.

Using a custom User Agent string or the system's User Agent string derived from urlmon.dll Adversaries may impersonate legitimate protocols or web service traffic to disguise command and control activity and thwart analysis efforts.

Uploading data in 2048-byte chunks An adversary may exfiltrate data in fixed size chunks instead of whole files or limit packet sizes below certain thresholds. This approach may be used to avoid triggering network data transfer threshold alerts.

Using a custom User Agent string or the system's User Agent string derived from urlmon.dll Adversaries may communicate using OSI application layer protocols to avoid detection/network filtering by blending in with existing traffic.

Appending a #le signature header to all encrypted data, prior to upload or download, by randomly selecting from the #le types: ICO GIF JPG PNG MP3 BMP Adversaries may match or approximate the name or location of legitimate files or resources when naming/placing them.

annotator L.report_text:
POSHSPY leverages two of the tools the group frequently uses

PowerShell The use of PowerShell to execute malicious commands or scripts. POSHSPY uses PowerShell as part of its toolkit.

Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) The use of WMI to maintain persistence on the system. POSHSPY leverages WMI as one of the main tools.

POSHSPY's use of WMI to both store and persist the backdoor code makes it nearly invisible to anyone not familiar with the intricacies of WMI The use of WMI to maintain persistence on the system. POSHSPY uses WMI to store and persist the backdoor code.

POSHSPY's use of WMI to both store and persist the backdoor code makes it nearly invisible to anyone not familiar with the intricacies of WMI The use of WMI to execute commands in order to avoid detection.

Its use of a PowerShell payload means that only legitimate system processes are utilized and that the malicious code execution can only be identi#ed through enhanced logging or in memory The use of PowerShell payloads allows only legitimate system processes to be used, making detection of malicious code more difficult.

tra$c obfuscation, Obfuscation of traffic and data.

Writing executables to a randomly-selected directory under Program Files, and naming the EXE to match the chosen directory name, or, if that fails, writing the executable to a systemgenerated temporary #le name, using the EXE extension This technique involves masking executable files with names and paths that seem legitimate or familiar to the user, such as using file names that correspond to random directory names under Program Files.

Modifying the Standard Information timestamps (created, modi#ed, accessed) of every downloaded executable to match a randomly selected #le from the System32 directory that was created prior to 2013 This TTP describes the action of modifying or manipulating file timestamps to hide malware activities, making it more difficult to detect and analyze operations performed on the system.

Encrypting communications using AES and RSA public key cryptography to the use of asymmetric encryption algorithms, such as RSA, and symmetric encryption algorithms, such as AES, to protect communications, making it more difficult for defenders to monitor and intercept communications.

Deriving C2 URLs from a Domain Generation Algorithm (DGA) dynamically generate a list of command and control (C2) domains on the fly, making it more difficult for defenders to block or detect C2 domains because they are randomly or pseudo-randomly generated.

Using a custom User Agent string or the system's User Agent string derived from urlmon.dll POSHSPY uses custom or system-derived User Agent strings and employs custom or randomly generated cookie names and values
